item.title: Re: Lunchely happenes
item.content:encoded: Big oof. I can't remember what that was supposed to be anymore. D:

item.title: Re: Lunchely happenes
item.content:encoded: “The URL you requested could not be found.”

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eating lead isn’t an option with this gun. I love the concept and I love the turn it takes after the test logs.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This went from fun test logs to HOLY SHIT

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, thanks. :) I really shouldn't complain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As the person who officially bumped it to +100 upvotes, I can say that I liked it because it was weird as fuck and the image of food flying out the back of someone's head whenever they shot themselves with a pistol was hilarious to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Kind of, but not? At least, not enough people saw a connection to keep it from inexplicably reaching +100.

The hell do people like about this crummy article, I stg c.c

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Isn't this kind of similar to SCP-1162?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "3118-1 are non anomalous"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Killing the child seems a bit drastic. I thought it was good enough just showing one of the D-classes was a cannibal

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The kid's an anomaly. It was created by an anomaly. You don't turn anomalies into personnel. That's my story and I'm sticking to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mostly liked this skip. It is short, simple, clinical, and then the test logs shoe how this effect can have a lot of impact in the lives of people, be it preventing a successful suicide or bringing the past back in a form that is as subtle and powerful as a remarkable lunch.

But I have two issues here. The first, as a lot of other, is with the termination of the child for no reason whatsoever. The child could be locked for further studies, or could be released, or even raised to be in the future a new Foundation asset. Heck, the child could be D-Classed. Or there could be a reason for them to do it, and the log actually reveal it.

The other issue is with the mortality rate. It simply seems unnecessary and forced. It is anomalous by itself, but plays no other role in the skip aside from being there. If you can get rid of an anomalous effect without loss to the skip, then you probably should.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It starts off all nice and good and then when it gets to the end, it gets really dark. Well done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hrm… I don't like the part where the foundation killed the resulting kid. Not because 'oh no they killed a poor child' but because my impression is that they would have more likely kept the kid around for study. In addition, the 'and then they killed the kid' honestly feels a bit like a throwback to older articles, it feels like a bit of tacked on shock value that really doesn't actually add much - especially on the tail end of the punchline.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There’s some horror in here (besides the obvious) where it states that any other part of the body besides the head results in normal trauma. The Foundation would have tested this several times to make sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I see TL333s point here. But it feels like two different foundations.

Also, why should the child even be alive? None of the food described earlier was recreated in a living form. If it was consumed in a still living state, which seems to be the most difficult watly, would it not be interesting to keep it as a further test subject? Or would it more emotional to have an incomplete but still living person produced that is terminated out of nessesity as it would not live long anyway?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was my reckoning when I wrote it.

I also hope it creates some cognitive dissonance when compared with the "they got the researcher help" test log…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I disagree with those that saying that the kid should have been let free. Having an exact replica of a child who was previously murdered walk around would just lead to more coverups and more resources consumed if anyone took notice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ultimately an upvote but the casual child-murder at the end almost kept me from doing so. I object to it on the grounds that the Foundation is ultimately pragmatic and amnesticizing the kid before putting them in an orphanage far away from the original instance seems far easier and cheaper than dealing with the backlash that comes from killing a harmless kid. Foundation employees are human, after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A Gun that, instead of hurting you if you get hit in the head, makes anything you've eaten just materlialize sounds pretty funny. lets see how they got this thing! Oh… Suicide.. well… atleast the guys getting help now. What about the test logs… Ehh.. thats cute… Oh… Oh my…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I swear to god, I have never seen a 40 oz. bottle of beer before, only cans, but then I don't drink the kind of beer that comes in 40's, so maybe I'm just dumb. Anyway, I'll change it, since a cursory Googling brings up only bottle choices!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some searching turns up at least one company, Faxe, that makes 1L cans, but it seems that's the largest commonly produced beverage can.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can confirm. I've never seen a 40 ounce can, sounds like it would be hard to drink without caving in the sides.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
40s are commonplace, but not 40 ounce cans.
Usually I think you'll find them in bottles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What Petrograd said, but the idea is sufficiently novel and otherwise well-executed that I'll no-vote rather than downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is so fucking weird and random. I love it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Getting shot in the head and the magical recreation of your lunch don't seem to be correlated in any way, but I think it works very well!
Main thing I like about this: You kept the firearm terminology to a minimum, and using the correct terms when necessary. It's exactly what a simple firearm SCP should be in my mind, minimal usage of complex terms and ideas for a simple route.

+1 from the Spaghett

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Now that was some well done mood whiplash. I was morbidly curious about what would happen if you used the anomaly on a cannibal, and then that happened.

+1 for a solid short skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The twist at the end was nice, but somehow what got my upvote was the initial discovery. That's the kind of imagery that makes this place special.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's alright; Abettik's upvote registered twice, so everything evens out (damn you wikidot).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I imagine if they were killed by it, then that's it, but otherwise, why not?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like that the kid at the end was terminated, but the fact that Kreighbaum got help is enough to overcome that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would 3118 work on corpses? Because if so, I have a tale brewing in my head…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Psst, Petrograd, your downvote registered twice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ah, I see. I read into that the wrong way. In that case, perhaps the final subject could have used an extra log or two to set that up:

If the log containing the dead test subject was placed directly before this point (basically, disperse D-10331'a entries throughout, instead of being consecutive, or simply move 'repeat tests' to right before the end), then from that they learn repeat tests are deadly…which makes finding volunteers harder…until they find the final subject.

So, maybe including procedures in which the SCPF will allow subjects to consume -1s if they so desire, it'd better establish that final subject and their motives beforehand. Like, this one D hears he can potentially blow his brains out with an anomaly, and risks it/is enthusiastic thinking he'll get another chance to…erm…blech!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Though terminating the little boy at the end seems a little more cruel than cold.

Plastic container with reheated slice of sausage and pepperoni pizza. 1.18 L can B████████ brand lager beer. Four F██████ R█████ hazelnut chocolates in warpper.

Are you kidding me with these redactions? Like who doesn't know what B████████ Beer is, and who else makes little hazelnut chocolates other than F██████ R█████? That's like saying "common hamburger chain B█████ K███." :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, I understand what you mean, but the article came as a sort of "That's it?" with a confused tone. I'm simply saying it didn't do much for me, and the shock factor felt a bit half-baked as there wasn't much build-up for the grand punchline.

3/10 I don't like whiplash :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ehhh. It's a cool mental image, but the logs didn't do much for me and the last one felt like just shock value. The tone kinda went slightly violent -> whimsical -> abruptly dark, without feeling coherent or worked in.
-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had no problem with it. I don't think everything needs that type of throughline - sometimes these things work by revealing things about people - which here runs the gamut from sweet and heartwarming to utterly depraved, and that's fine. It doesn't need to tell one anything else about the anomaly, because here it is about the subjects.

Adding anomalous effects to something which is already pretty original is simply stretching the WTF factor unnecessarily.

10/10 would mood-whiplash again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You've got it! And no, that's a 40 ounce beer. Is that not a thing by you, I guess?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea is very interesting, the set-up for the 'punchline' (as I like to call it) is nice, but I feel like the actual punchline itself is very… anticlimactic, for what I look for. The last D subject is a cannibal, but that doesn't tell me anything else. The plot-line is expressed through the tests, but I don't think it conveys as much meaning and gravity as it could have, if expressed through a different medium.

I feel if perhaps there was an added effect that the Foundation found out about through the tests, then went into further elaboration and construction of a more fulfilling end, perhaps then I would find it more fascinating, but right now, it's a -1 for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From what I understand...
– hide block

The gun produces meals that have great emotional significance to the person shot, and if one goes too far back in time with the lunch, the subject being shot dies?

This is great, one thing though:

1.18 L can B████████ brand lager beer.

That is a comically large beer can. 1.18 liters is almost five cups, or 40 fl oz; I think you missed the m in mL.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, nice! You finally got this thing on the mainlist; it's cleaned up rather well. Just as short and sweet as I remember. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Oh, gun that makes food from the past. That's weird. But funny! I like these testing l— oh."

That's some real good shit my man, wasn't expecting it. In hindsight, I really should've. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How appropriate that the last test log hits the reader like a gunshot. +1

item.title: Lunchely happenes
item.content:encoded:
Thanks to weryllium, ResearcherCato and Roget for feedback, and thanks also to Glorpman for being a helpful ant enthusiast.

+ Click for dumb inspiration
- But I'm warning you

It's really stupid.
